explanation: Your body burns food all day to give you power to move. After a while, the food you ate gets used up inside. Your empty belly sends a signal up to your brain. The brain reads it and makes you feel that hungry pang. Eating fills you back up and stops the hungry feeling.

A corn seed has a drop of water shut in a hard case. When the stove heats it, that water turns into steam. The steam pushes hard on the shell from the inside. The shell can only take so much before it bursts. The kernel flips inside out with a loud little pop.

Bones make up the hard frame that holds your body up. Without them your body would flop like a soft rag doll. Your bones give your muscles a firm place to pull against. They also guard soft parts, like the ribs around your heart. Strong bones let you stand, walk, run, and jump.

concept: How does soap clean our hands?
phrasing: How does soap clean our hands?
explanation: Dirt and grease cling to your skin and water alone slides past them. Soap is built to grab grease on one end and water on the other. Its grabby ends latch onto the grease stuck to your hands. When you rinse, the water pulls the soap and grease away. Your hands come out clean because the soap dragged the mess off.

A bird needs a safe, snug spot to keep its eggs warm. It hunts for twigs, grass, mud, and soft bits of fluff. The bird weaves the twigs into a bowl with its beak and feet. It lines the inside with the soft stuff to cushion the eggs. The finished nest holds the eggs safe until they hatch.

A rubber ball is springy, so it can squish and spring back. When it drops on the floor, the bottom squashes flat a bit. The squashed rubber pushes hard to snap back to its round shape. That push shoves the ball up off the ground again. The ball bounces less each time until it comes to a stop.

concept: Why does the ocean have waves?
phrasing: Why does the ocean have waves?
explanation: Most waves in the sea start from wind that blows across it. The moving air drags on the top and lifts it into bumps. Each bump rolls along as a wave that heads to the shore. The water mostly bobs in place as the wave moves through it. When a wave meets the shallow sand, it tips over and breaks.

A plant sips water from the soil using its thin roots. The water creeps into tiny tubes that run up the stem. It climbs the tubes all the way up to the leaves. The plant uses that water to make food and stay firm. Sun on the leaves pulls more water up, like a straw.

A yawn is a big, slow breath you take with a wide mouth. You often yawn when you feel tired or worn out. The deep breath may pull in extra air to wake you up. It may also help cool your busy brain down a bit. That is why a good yawn can leave you feeling fresher.

concept: How does rust form on metal?
phrasing: How does rust form on metal?
explanation: Rust is the crumbly orange coat that forms on old iron. It grows when iron is left out in both water and air. Bit by bit, the iron joins with the air to turn into rust. Water speeds this up, so wet metal rusts much faster. A coat of paint keeps the air and water off the metal.

concept: What makes a sound loud or soft?
phrasing: What makes a sound loud or soft?
explanation: A sound starts when a thing shakes and stirs the air into waves. A big, strong shake pushes tall waves through the air. Those tall waves hit your ears and you hear a loud sound. A small, gentle shake makes short little waves instead. Short waves are weaker, so the sound comes through soft.

concept: Why does the moon shine at night?
phrasing: Why does the moon shine at night?
explanation: The moon cannot make any light of its own at all. It shines because the sun lights up one side of it. That bright side bounces sunlight all the way to us. We see that bounced light shining back as the glow of the moon. When clouds hide the moon, the bounced light cannot reach us.
